content.text: Async & Long-Running Tools

Tool calls are synchronous by default: the model issues a call, execution blocks, and the result flows back before the model continues. This works for fast operations, but breaks down for tasks that take seconds to minutes — a PDF report, a database migration, a video transcode. The solution is the enqueue-and-poll pattern: one tool starts the job and returns a handle immediately, a second tool checks status, and a third retrieves the result when ready.

attrs.alt: Async tool pattern: start_job returns job_id, model polls get_job_status until DONE, then calls get_job_result

content.text: Never block the agent loop for more than ~10 seconds. If an operation might take longer, expose it as start/status/result — three separate tools — so the model can interleave other work while waiting.
In-Process Job Runner

For jobs that can complete within a single process, a background thread is sufficient. The handler starts the thread, registers the job ID, and returns immediately. Subsequent calls check the thread's state.
Tool Schema for the Three-Tool Pattern
The descriptions must tell the model when to call each tool. Without clear instructions, the model may call
get_report_result
before the job is done, or forget to poll at all.
Timeout and Stuck-Job Detection
A job that runs longer than expected (infinite loop, deadlock, external dependency outage) must be detected and cancelled. Add a max-duration field to the job and check it during status queries.
Testing Async Tools

import pytest, time

def test_generate_report_returns_job_id_immediately():
    t0 = time.time()
    result = generate_report("sales_q2")
    elapsed = time.time() - t0
    assert "job_id" in result
    assert elapsed < 0.5   # must return within 500 ms

def test_status_progresses_to_done():
    result = generate_report("sales_q2")
    job_id = result["job_id"]
    for _ in range(20):
        status = get_report_status(job_id)
        if status["status"] == "DONE":
            break
        time.sleep(0.5)
    assert status["status"] == "DONE"

def test_result_unavailable_before_done():
    result = generate_report("sales_q2")
    job_id = result["job_id"]
    immediate = get_report_result(job_id)
    # Job is still running; result should not be available yet
    assert immediate.get("error") in ("NOT_READY", None) or immediate.get("status") == "DONE"

def test_unknown_job_returns_not_found():
    result = get_report_status("no-such-job")
    assert result["error"] == "NOT_FOUND"

def test_stuck_job_detected():
    global MAX_JOB_SECONDS
    orig = MAX_JOB_SECONDS
    MAX_JOB_SECONDS = 0   # force immediate timeout
    result = generate_report("stuck_test")
    job_id = result["job_id"]
    time.sleep(0.1)
    status = get_report_status(job_id)
    assert status["status"] == "FAILED"
    assert "limit" in status.get("error_detail", "")
    MAX_JOB_SECONDS = orig
Summary
Never block the agent loop for more than ~10 seconds. Expose long-running work as three tools: start, status, and result.
The start tool returns a
job_id
immediately. The status tool returns PENDING | RUNNING | DONE | FAILED. The result tool returns the output only when status is DONE.
Write the tool descriptions to explicitly instruct the model to poll status before fetching results — the model will not infer this automatically.
Add a wall-clock timeout to every job. Detect stuck jobs during status queries and mark them FAILED rather than leaving them in a RUNNING state indefinitely.
Test that the start tool returns within 500 ms even when the underlying work takes several seconds.
